title: DSA Complete Path
description: Learn problem solving from basics: arrays, strings, recursion, trees, graphs and dynamic programming.
overview: DSA helps you solve coding problems in a planned way. You learn how to store data, search faster, reduce repeated work and choose the right approach.

sections.heading: Arrays and strings
sections.body: Arrays store many values in order. Strings are text values that can be checked, reversed, counted or split into smaller parts.

sections.heading: Recursion
sections.body: Recursion means a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Always define the stopping condition first.

sections.heading: Trees and graphs
sections.body: Trees help represent hierarchy. Graphs help represent connections like roads, networks, friendships and dependencies.

sections.heading: Dynamic programming
sections.body: Dynamic programming stores answers to smaller problems so the program does not calculate the same thing again and again.

example: Example: To find the largest number in an array, keep one variable called largest and update it while scanning each item.
practice: Find the smallest number in an array.
Solve one recursion problem like factorial.
Draw a tree traversal by hand.

quiz.question: Why do we use arrays?
quiz.answer: To store multiple values in one ordered collection.

quiz.question: What must every recursion problem have?
quiz.answer: A base case that stops the function.

title: System Design Basics
description: Understand clean design, common patterns, APIs, caching and how to explain your design in interviews.
overview: System design teaches you how to plan an application before building it. You think about users, APIs, data, speed, security and scaling.

sections.heading: Understand the requirement
sections.body: First ask what the system should do. A chat app, food app and learning app all need different features and data.

sections.heading: Design the API
sections.body: APIs are the doors through which frontend and backend talk. Keep names simple, like GET courses or POST login.

sections.heading: Store the data
sections.body: Choose tables or collections based on the data. Users, courses, lessons, quizzes and progress usually need separate storage.

sections.heading: Scale slowly
sections.body: Start with a simple design. Add caching, queues and load balancing only when the app really needs them.

example: Example: A course platform needs user login, course list, lesson content, progress tracking and certificate generation.
practice: List APIs for a notes app.
Draw the database tables for a quiz app.

quiz.question: What should you clarify first in system design?
quiz.answer: The requirements and main users.

quiz.question: Why do we use caching?
quiz.answer: To make frequently used data load faster.

title: C++ Programming Basics
description: Start coding with variables, loops, functions, pointers, OOP and simple practice problems.
overview: C++ is a fast programming language often used for DSA. Start with syntax, variables, conditions, loops and functions.

sections.heading: Variables
sections.body: Variables store values. Use int for whole numbers, double for decimals, char for one character and string for text.

sections.heading: Conditions
sections.body: Use if, else if and else when your program needs to make decisions.

sections.heading: Loops
sections.body: Loops repeat work. Use for loops when you know the count and while loops when the count depends on a condition.

sections.heading: Functions
sections.body: Functions keep code organized. A function should do one clear job and return a useful result when needed.

example: Example: int total = a + b; stores the sum of two numbers in total.
practice: Check if a number is even.
Write a function that returns the larger of two numbers.

quiz.question: Which type stores whole numbers in C++?
quiz.answer: int.

quiz.question: Why do we use functions?
quiz.answer: To reuse code and keep programs organized.

title: Java Programming Basics
description: Learn Java in a simple way with classes, objects, collections and interview-friendly exercises.
overview: Java is used for apps, backend systems and interviews. Learn classes, objects, methods, arrays and collections step by step.

sections.heading: Class and object
sections.body: A class is a blueprint. An object is a real item made from that blueprint.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.body: Methods are actions inside a class. They help keep logic grouped and reusable.

sections.heading: Collections
sections.body: Collections like ArrayList and HashMap help store and manage groups of values.

sections.heading: Exception handling
sections.body: Exceptions are errors that happen while the program runs. Use try and catch to handle them cleanly.

example: Example: A Student class can have name, roll number and a method called printDetails.
practice: Store five names in an ArrayList.
Use a HashMap to store subject marks.

quiz.question: What is an object?
quiz.answer: A real instance created from a class.

quiz.question: What does ArrayList store?
quiz.answer: A list of values that can grow or shrink.

title: HTML
description: Complete HTML and Web Development Foundations Handbook1 covers internet basics, document anatomy, semantic HTML, forms, media, accessibility and SEO-friendly page structure.
overview: HTML gives structure to a webpage. It tells the browser what each part means: heading, paragraph, image, form, button or section.

sections.heading: Page structure
sections.body: A good page has clear headings, sections, navigation and meaningful content. This helps users and search engines.

sections.heading: Text and media
sections.body: Use headings for titles, paragraphs for text, lists for grouped points, images for visuals and links for navigation.

sections.heading: Forms
sections.body: Forms collect user input. Use labels so users know what each input field is for.

sections.heading: Accessibility
sections.body: Write HTML that screen readers can understand. Use alt text for images and buttons with clear names.

practice: Add a contact form with labels.
Use semantic tags like header, main and footer.

quiz.question: What is HTML used for?
quiz.answer: To structure content on a webpage.

quiz.question: Why should inputs have labels?
quiz.answer: So users understand what information to enter.

title: CSS
description: Responsive layout, Grid, Flexbox, tokens, animations and modern component styling.
overview: CSS controls how a website looks. It handles colors, spacing, layout, typography, responsiveness and animation.

sections.heading: Selectors
sections.body: Selectors choose which HTML elements to style. You can select by element, class, id or state.

sections.heading: Box model
sections.body: Every element has content, padding, border and margin. Understanding this makes layout easier.

sections.heading: Flexbox and grid
sections.body: Flexbox is great for one-direction layouts. Grid is great for rows and columns.

sections.heading: Responsive design
sections.body: Responsive design makes the page look good on mobile, tablet and desktop screens.

practice: Make a card stack on mobile.

quiz.question: What does CSS control?
quiz.answer: The visual style and layout of a webpage.

quiz.question: When is CSS grid useful?
quiz.answer: When you need rows and columns.

title: JavaScript
description: DOM, async programming, APIs, testing, modules and interview-grade problem solving.
overview: JavaScript makes websites interactive. You can react to clicks, update content, call APIs and handle data.

sections.heading: Variables and types
sections.body: Use variables to store values like numbers, strings, booleans, arrays and objects.

sections.heading: Functions
sections.body: Functions group code into reusable actions. Give them clear names based on what they do.

sections.heading: DOM
sections.body: The DOM is the page structure that JavaScript can read and change.

sections.heading: Async code
sections.body: Async code is used when work takes time, like calling an API or loading data.

practice: Fetch sample data from an API.

quiz.question: What does JavaScript add to a webpage?
quiz.answer: Interactivity and logic.

quiz.question: What is the DOM?
quiz.answer: The page structure that JavaScript can change.

title: React Product Engineering
description: Hooks, server rendering, state, forms, performance and production app architecture.
overview: React helps you build user interfaces with reusable components. Each component owns a small part of the screen.

sections.heading: Components
sections.body: Components are reusable UI blocks. A button, navbar, course card or dashboard widget can be a component.

sections.heading: Props
sections.body: Props pass data into a component. They make one component reusable with different content.

sections.heading: State
sections.body: State stores values that can change, like form input, theme mode or selected tab.

sections.heading: Effects
sections.body: Effects run code after rendering, such as reading local storage or loading data.

practice: Make a tabs component for lessons.

quiz.question: What is a React component?
quiz.answer: A reusable piece of UI.

quiz.question: What is state used for?
quiz.answer: Data that changes while the app is running.

title: Node.js API Studio
description: Express APIs, JWT auth, validation, databases, logging and deployment workflows.
overview: Node.js lets you run JavaScript on the server. With Express, you can create APIs for login, courses, quizzes and dashboards.

sections.heading: Routes
sections.body: Routes define what happens when a user or frontend visits an API URL.

sections.heading: Middleware
sections.body: Middleware runs before the final route. It can check auth, parse JSON or log requests.

sections.heading: Authentication
sections.body: JWT auth sends a token after login. The token proves the user is signed in.

sections.heading: Database connection
sections.body: The API can read and write data from MongoDB or PostgreSQL depending on your project.

quiz.question: What is Express used for?
quiz.answer: Creating server routes and APIs.

quiz.question: What does a JWT token prove?
quiz.answer: That a user has logged in.

title: Python Backend Development
overview: This course is based on your book Python Backend Development. It teaches backend engineering step by step, from Python basics to production APIs, databases, security, deployment, microservices and a real backend project.

sections.heading: Python foundations
sections.body: Start with Python setup, variables, functions, data structures, object-oriented programming, error handling, logging, file handling and clean coding habits for backend work.

sections.heading: Web and API fundamentals
sections.body: Learn how the internet works, how HTTP requests and responses move between client and server, and why APIs are the main connection between frontend apps and backend systems.

sections.heading: FastAPI development
sections.body: Build Python APIs with FastAPI. Create routes, handle request data, validate input with Pydantic, return JSON responses and structure backend code clearly.

sections.heading: Databases and data management
sections.body: Understand relational databases, PostgreSQL, SQL queries, schema design and ORM usage so your backend can store and read data safely.

sections.heading: Security and authentication
sections.body: Learn login systems, authorization, password protection, tokens, secure API access and common backend security practices.

sections.heading: Performance and reliability
sections.body: Study caching, background tasks, queues, async programming, testing, debugging, API documentation and performance optimization.

sections.heading: Deployment and architecture
sections.body: Package apps with Docker, understand DevOps basics, deploy backend applications, design production-ready APIs, explore microservices and complete a real project.

chapters: Chapter 2 - Python Environment Setup for Backend Development
Chapter 3 - Python Fundamentals for Backend Development
Chapter 7 - File Handling and Data Processing
Chapter 8 - Understanding the Internet and HTTP

outline.part: Part IV - Databases and Data Management
chapters: Chapter 15 - Working with PostgreSQL and SQL

outline.part: Part VII - Testing and System Reliability

example: Example: A FastAPI backend receives a request, validates the input, runs business logic, talks to PostgreSQL, and returns a clean JSON response to the frontend.
practice: Create a FastAPI route called /health that returns { status: 'ok' }.
Design tables for users, courses, lessons and progress.
Explain the request-response cycle in your own words.
Write three security rules for a login API.
Plan a Docker deployment checklist for a backend app.

quiz.question: What is the main job of a backend?
quiz.answer: It handles data, business logic, security and communication between the app and database.

quiz.question: Why is FastAPI useful for Python backend development?
quiz.answer: It helps build APIs quickly with routing, validation, automatic docs and async support.

quiz.question: Why do backend apps use databases?
quiz.answer: To store important data like users, courses, orders, progress and settings.

quiz.question: What does Docker help with?
quiz.answer: It packages the app and dependencies so the backend runs consistently in different environments.

title: Python for Data Analysis
description: Python, NumPy, Pandas, notebooks, data cleaning and dashboard-ready analysis.
overview: Python is useful for data analysis because it is simple to read and has strong libraries like Pandas, NumPy and Matplotlib.

sections.heading: Load data
sections.body: Start by loading data from CSV, Excel or a database. Always inspect the first few rows.

sections.heading: Clean data
sections.body: Fix missing values, remove duplicates and convert columns into the right type.

sections.heading: Analyze data
sections.body: Group, filter and summarize data to answer useful questions.

sections.heading: Visualize data
sections.body: Charts make patterns easier to see. Use bar charts, line charts and scatter plots.

example: df.groupby('course')['revenue'].sum() shows total revenue for each course.
practice: Create a bar chart for top categories.

quiz.question: Why do analysts clean data?
quiz.answer: To make results accurate and reliable.

quiz.question: What does Pandas help with?
quiz.answer: Working with tables of data.

title: SQL Practice Lab
description: Queries, joins, windows, CTEs, analytics questions and interview SQL drills.
overview: SQL is used to ask questions from tables. You can filter, sort, group, join and summarize data.

sections.heading: SELECT
sections.body: SELECT chooses the columns you want to see from a table.

sections.heading: WHERE
sections.body: WHERE filters rows based on a condition, like price greater than 500.

sections.heading: JOIN
sections.body: JOIN combines data from two or more tables using a matching column.

sections.heading: GROUP BY
sections.body: GROUP BY helps summarize rows, like total sales by month or users by country.

example: SELECT course, COUNT(*) FROM enrollments GROUP BY course;

quiz.question: What does WHERE do?
quiz.answer: It filters rows.

quiz.question: Why do we use JOIN?
quiz.answer: To combine related tables.

title: CS Core Interview Kit
description: Prepare the core topics: operating systems, DBMS, OOP, networking and common placement questions.
overview: CS core topics help in interviews and exams. Focus on DBMS, operating systems, OOP and computer networks.

sections.heading: DBMS
sections.body: DBMS teaches how data is stored, related, protected and queried.

sections.heading: Operating systems
sections.body: OS topics include processes, threads, memory, scheduling and deadlocks.

sections.heading: OOP
sections.body: OOP uses classes and objects to organize code. Learn inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction and encapsulation.

sections.heading: Computer networks
sections.body: Networks explain how data moves between devices using protocols like HTTP, TCP and IP.

example: Example: In OOP, a Vehicle class can be inherited by Car and Bike classes.

quiz.question: What does DBMS manage?
quiz.answer: Stored data and database operations.

quiz.question: What is encapsulation?
quiz.answer: Keeping data and methods together with controlled access.

title: Machine Learning Launchpad
description: Regression, classification, evaluation, feature engineering and model demos.
overview: Machine learning helps computers learn patterns from data. You train a model, test it and use it for predictions.

sections.heading: Dataset
sections.body: A dataset contains examples. Each row is usually one example and each column is a feature.

sections.heading: Training
sections.body: Training means the model studies data and learns patterns from it.

sections.heading: Evaluation
sections.body: Evaluation checks how well the model works on data it has not seen before.

sections.heading: Prediction
sections.body: After training, the model can predict values or classes for new data.

example: Example: A model can learn past customer data and predict whether a customer may leave.
practice: List features for a student score model.
Split data into train and test sets.

quiz.question: What is training?
quiz.answer: The process where a model learns patterns from data.

quiz.question: Why do we test a model?
quiz.answer: To check performance on new data.

title: Deep Learning Visualized
description: Neural networks, backpropagation, CNNs, transformers and animated experiments.
overview: Deep learning uses neural networks with many layers. It is useful for images, text, speech and complex patterns.

sections.heading: Neurons
sections.body: A neuron takes inputs, applies weights and produces an output.

sections.heading: Layers
sections.body: Layers pass information forward. Earlier layers learn simple patterns and later layers learn deeper patterns.

sections.heading: Backpropagation
sections.body: Backpropagation helps the network adjust weights when the prediction is wrong.

sections.heading: Applications
sections.body: Deep learning is used in image recognition, chatbots, translation and recommendation systems.

example: Example: An image model may learn edges first, then shapes, then full objects.
practice: List two deep learning use cases.

quiz.question: What is a neuron?
quiz.answer: A small unit that receives inputs and produces an output.

quiz.question: What does backpropagation do?
quiz.answer: It adjusts weights to reduce errors.
